required.title: [Research] FDA Food Code and/or local health department regulations

suggested.title: [Video] Tour a commercial kitchen or food production facility

competence_markers.description: The student has designed and implemented a household cleaning system with tasks, schedule, assignments, and standards
The student can navigate shared-space cleaning dynamics productively — explicit standards, early communication, systems over nagging
The student understands institutional sanitation standards and can connect them to household practices
The student has assessed and improved their home's environmental management (air quality, mold prevention, ventilation)
The student can teach cleaning skills to others and delegate tasks with clear standards
The student manages household cleaning as a system, not a series of individual tasks

cross_connections.description: Environmental management overlaps heavily — moisture control prevents both mold (2.3.2) and structural damage (2.3.3). These two sub-domains should be coordinated.
Shared space negotiation is a relationship skill. How you handle cleaning disagreements predicts how you handle other domestic disagreements.
Designing and maintaining systems for others requires both character (holding fair standards, addressing issues honestly) and discipline (consistent follow-through).

Institutional sanitation standards are public health regulation in action. The student who understands why restaurants are inspected understands something about the role of government in public safety.

content.why_this_matters:
You can clean a house to a high standard. Now the question is: can you manage cleanliness as a system — for a household, for shared living spaces, and potentially at an institutional level? Can you design a cleaning routine, delegate tasks, hold standards, and handle the inevitable social friction that comes from shared spaces?

This is where cleaning becomes management. The skills shift from execution (you scrub the bathroom) to system design (you create the schedule, assign responsibilities, set standards, and ensure they are met). This is the same shift that happens in every domain at Standing Ground — from competent individual to someone who can build and manage systems that work reliably even when they are not personally doing every task.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Household maintenance system design** — creating cleaning schedules, assigning responsibilities, setting standards for a household
2. **Shared space management** — navigating the social dynamics of cleaning when others do not share your standards
3. **Institutional sanitation understanding** — how hospitals, restaurants, and facilities maintain cleanliness and why it matters
4. **Environmental management** — indoor air quality, mold prevention, moisture control, seasonal maintenance
5. **Teaching and delegating** — the ability to teach cleaning skills and delegate with clear standards and accountability
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Designing a Household Cleaning System

A cleaning system has four components: tasks, schedule, assignments, and standards.

**Tasks:** Every recurring cleaning task in the household, organized by frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, seasonal). You built this list at Weathering Storms. At Standing Ground, you design it for the entire household — including tasks others will perform.

**Schedule:** When each task happens. The schedule must be realistic — it accounts for the household's actual rhythm, not an idealized version. A family with two working parents and three kids has a different schedule capacity than a retired couple.

**Assignments:** Who does what. This requires honest assessment of each household member's competence, schedule, and willingness. The system fails if assignments are unrealistic. A seven-year-old can wipe counters and take out trash. They cannot deep-clean a bathroom. Assign accordingly.

**Standards:** What "done" looks like. This is the most commonly missing component. Without clear standards, "I cleaned the kitchen" means different things to different people. Define it: counters wiped, stovetop wiped, dishes done, floor swept, trash out. If the standard is written and visible, disputes decrease and compliance increases.

**The management challenge:** Designing the system is the easy part. Managing it — holding standards when others slack, addressing failures without creating household conflict, adjusting the system when it is not working — is the real skill. This connects directly to leadership as studied in IC1 and practiced in every group endeavor.

### Part 2: Shared Space Dynamics

Roommates, siblings, spouses, and family members rarely share identical cleaning standards. This is one of the most common sources of household friction, and learning to navigate it is a life skill.

**Productive approaches:**
- **Explicit standards, not implicit expectations.** "I expect the kitchen to be clean" means nothing if your definition of clean differs from your roommate's. "Dishes washed and put away, counters wiped, stovetop clean, floor swept — by 9 PM" is a standard that can be met or not met.
- **Systems over nagging.** If dishes are chronically unwashed, the problem is not laziness (usually) — it is the absence of a system. A dishwasher running after dinner, a rotation of who loads and unloads, a rule that dishes do not sit overnight — systems solve problems that willpower alone does not.
- **Address early, not late.** A small cleaning disagreement discussed calmly in week one of living together is a minor conversation. The same issue addressed after six months of resentment is a relationship crisis. Address early.
- **Model the standard.** You cannot effectively hold others to a standard you do not meet yourself. Maintain your own standard first. Others often rise to match it — not always, but more often than you would expect.

### Part 3: Institutional Sanitation

Understanding how professional environments maintain cleanliness puts household cleaning in perspective and opens pathways to understanding public health infrastructure.

**Hospital sanitation:** Hospitals maintain some of the strictest sanitation protocols in any environment. Healthcare-associated infections kill tens of thousands of Americans annually. The protocols that prevent them — hand hygiene compliance, surface disinfection, sterilization of instruments, isolation procedures — are studied, measured, and enforced. When they fail, people die. This is cleaning at the highest stakes.

**Restaurant sanitation:** Health department inspections evaluate food service establishments on: food temperature control, cross-contamination prevention, employee hygiene, surface sanitation, pest control, and waste management. A restaurant that fails inspection can be shut down. Understanding these standards gives you the framework for evaluating any food preparation environment — including your own kitchen.

**Lessons for the household:** You are not running a hospital or a restaurant. But the principles transfer: routine beats reaction, standards must be defined to be met, the places you cannot see (behind appliances, inside drains, under sinks) matter as much as the places you can, and the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.

### Part 4: Environmental Management

The home is an environment. Managing that environment proactively prevents problems that are expensive and difficult to solve reactively.

**Indoor air quality:** The air inside most homes is 2-5 times more polluted than outdoor air (EPA). Sources: cooking fumes, cleaning product chemicals, mold spores, dust, pet dander, off-gassing from furniture and flooring. Mitigation: ventilation (open windows regularly), range hood use during cooking, HEPA air filters, controlling humidity, avoiding heavily scented products.

**Mold prevention:** Mold grows where moisture persists. Bathrooms, basements, window frames, and areas around plumbing fixtures are primary risk zones. Prevention: ventilate bathrooms during and after showers, fix leaks immediately, maintain humidity below 60%, clean visible mold promptly with appropriate products. Mold that has penetrated building materials (drywall, wood framing) requires professional remediation — this connects to 2.3.3 (Household Systems).

**Seasonal maintenance:** Spring deep-cleaning is tradition for a reason — winter seal-up creates accumulated indoor pollution that a thorough cleaning and airing resolves. Fall preparation includes weatherproofing, which prevents the moisture intrusion that causes winter mold. The cleaning calendar and the maintenance calendar (2.3.3) should align.

### Part 5: Teaching and Delegating

The Standing Ground student can teach a younger student or household member to clean properly and can delegate household tasks with clear standards and accountability.

**Teaching cleaning skills:** Demonstrate, then supervise, then inspect. Show the correct technique once. Watch the learner do it. Inspect the result and give specific feedback: "The sink is good. The mirror has streaks — here is how to avoid that." Do not redo their work in front of them unless the failure is significant. Let them correct their own work after feedback.

**Delegating with standards:** When you assign a cleaning task to someone else, give them three things: what the task is, when it is due, and what "done" looks like. Then inspect. If the standard is met, acknowledge it. If not, redirect to the standard without doing the work yourself. This is basic management — and it is harder than doing the cleaning yourself, which is why many people default to doing everything rather than developing others.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. System Design Quality
**Developing well:** The system is realistic, documented, and functioning. Assignments match capabilities. Standards are clear. The student adjusts the system when it is not working.

**Developing slowly:** The system exists on paper but is not followed. Assignments are unrealistic. Standards are vague.

### 2. Social Navigation
**Developing well:** The student can discuss cleaning standards with household members without creating conflict. They address issues directly and early. They model the standard they expect.

**Developing slowly:** The student avoids confrontation (and cleans everything themselves) or creates conflict by holding others to standards without diplomacy.

### 3. Teaching Effectiveness
**Developing well:** The student can teach a younger person to clean a room to standard. They demonstrate, supervise, inspect, and give constructive feedback.

**Developing slowly:** The student takes over when the learner struggles or gives vague feedback ("do it better").
